system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 19: 4/4 rhythms over 3/4

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 19 — 4/4 rhythms over 3/4

_Pages 81-84._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Rhythms duration relationship

> these rhythms are twice as long as the ones in Chapter 20.

**Significance (p. 81).** Structural relationship between chapters.

## Five time zones / gears

> In this chapter we will consider five different time zones or gears to play in.

**Significance (p. 82).** Taxonomy of five rhythmic subdivisions as interchangeable gears.

## Adapting lines to time zones

> The aim of this chapter is to expand your vocabulary by adapting lines to these varying time zones
> or gears.

**Significance (p. 82).** Vocabulary expansion through gear-switching.

## Triplet gear note truncation rule

> To make this line feel comfortable
> in triplet gear you need to leave off the first four or the last four notes. In this case we'll leave off
> the last four.

**Significance (p. 83).** Mechanical rule for fitting eight-note phrase into triplet subdivision.

## Metronome and articulation reference

> Set your metronome between 50 and 60 for the half note and play the line in its origi-
> nal form. While playing the line as eighth notes take notice of your articulations and accents.

**Significance (p. 83).** Tempo prescription and articulation/accent baseline.

## Groove internalization method

> Continue repeating the line until you are in the groove of triplets and phrasing them as if they
> were eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 83).** Phrasing triplets with eighth-note articulation logic.

## Quarter-note triplet repetition drill

> Next try playing the line as quarter-note triplets. Again repeat the line over and over until you feel
> that groove.

**Significance (p. 84).** Gear-shifting via repetition.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
